title: Why this chronicle matters now
summary: Eric C. Greene's research program continues to advance our understanding of genome integrity, DNA repair, and the molecular mechanisms that maintain genetic stability—fundamental questions with direct implications for cancer biology and human health.

signals.label: Genome Integrity Research
signals.text: The Greene Lab's work on DNA repair mechanisms and homologous recombination provides fundamental insights into how cells maintain genome stability, with direct relevance to understanding cancer development and potential therapeutic approaches.

signals.label: Single-Molecule Biophysics
signals.text: Pioneering techniques like DNA curtains have enabled researchers worldwide to visualize molecular processes in real-time, advancing the field of single-molecule biophysics and revealing dynamics invisible to traditional biochemistry.

signals.label: Cancer Center Connections
signals.text: As a member of Columbia's Herbert Irving Comprehensive Cancer Center, Greene's research bridges fundamental biophysics with clinically relevant questions about genome stability and disease mechanisms.
